title: Claude Code Plugins
subtitle: The plugin.json manifest, the skills/commands/agents/hooks/.mcp.json components, and testing via --plugin-dir

theory.text: A plugin — packaging for everything at once

theory.md:
By this lesson you have a whole zoo of extensions in your arsenal: skills (world 3), slash commands and hooks (world 5), MCP servers (this world). One last problem remains — **delivery**. How do you hand a colleague "my skill + my command + my MCP server" in one move, rather than a five-step instruction?

The answer is a **plugin**. A Claude Code plugin is a folder that bundles any combination of extensions: skills, commands, agents, hooks, and MCP servers. It installs with one command, updates centrally, and works the same for everyone. If an MCP server is a part, then a plugin is a box of parts plus the instructions.

When to build a plugin, and when a "loose scatter" is enough? A simple guideline: if an extension is needed only by you and only in one project — a local skill or `claude mcp add` is enough. As soon as the word "share" appears — with a colleague, another team, the community — it's time to package it into a plugin: it has a name, a version, and an author, which means it can be installed, updated, and discussed as a single whole.

theory.title: Anatomy of a plugin
theory.code:
my-plugin/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   └── plugin.json        ← manifest (the only required file)
├── skills/                ← skills (folders with SKILL.md)
│   └── report-style/
│       └── SKILL.md
├── commands/              ← slash commands (Markdown files)
│   └── weekly.md          ← becomes the /weekly command
├── agents/                ← custom subagents
│   └── reviewer.md
├── hooks/
│   └── hooks.json         ← hooks (auto-actions on events)
└── .mcp.json             ← the plugin's MCP servers

theory.md:
Remember two rules of geography:

1. **The manifest lives in the service folder** `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` — and only it. This is the plugin's "passport."
2. **All components live in the plugin root**: `skills/`, `commands/`, `agents/`, `hooks/`, `.mcp.json` — next to the `.claude-plugin/` folder, not inside it. Putting `commands/` inside `.claude-plugin/` is the most common beginner mistake: the plugin formally installs, but the commands don't appear, and figuring out why without knowing this rule is nearly impossible.

All components are optional: a plugin can consist of a single skill or a single command — and that's fine. The loader simply looks at which of the known folders and files exist in the root and connects what it finds: found `commands/` — registered commands, found `.mcp.json` — brought up the servers. You don't need to declare anything extra — the folder structure is the declaration.

theory.title: .claude-plugin/plugin.json — a minimal manifest

theory.md:
There's exactly one required field in the manifest — **`name`**: lowercase, words separated by hyphens (kebab-case), no spaces. The rest — description, version, author — are formally optional, but for publishing they're required in human terms: without them a user won't understand what they're installing, and without a version they won't see that an update was released.

What each component gives after the plugin is installed:

rows: Skills — knowledge and instructions for the agent
a skill "format reports by our template"
MCP servers brought up together with the plugin

theory.md:
How do you test a plugin before any publishing? The **`--plugin-dir`** flag: run `claude --plugin-dir ./my-plugin` — and Claude Code loads the plugin straight from a local folder, with no installation and no marketplace.

The plugin development cycle looks like this: edited the files → restarted the session with `--plugin-dir` → checked that the commands show up in `/help`, the skills fire, and the MCP servers came up (`/mcp`). The local run is honest: the plugin behaves exactly as it would after a real installation, so everything you debugged via `--plugin-dir` will work the same for your users. When it all works locally — the plugin is ready for the next step: the marketplace (next lesson).

theory.bad:
my-plugin/
├── plugin.json            ← manifest in the root
└── .claude-plugin/
    └── commands/
        └── weekly.md      ← commands inside the service folder
theory.good:
my-plugin/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   └── plugin.json        ← manifest in the service folder
└── commands/
    └── weekly.md          ← components in the plugin root
theory.explanation: Here everything is swapped: the manifest must live at .claude-plugin/plugin.json, and the components — in the plugin root. In the "bad" version Claude Code finds neither the manifest (it's not in the service folder) nor the /weekly command (it's hidden inside .claude-plugin/). File placement in a plugin isn't a matter of taste but a contract by which the loader reads it.

theory.md: The Claude Code documentation (the Plugins page) fixes the contract: a plugin's only required file is the manifest `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` with a `name` field; all components — skills, commands, agents, hooks, and MCP servers — are optional and located in the plugin root. For local development and testing, running with the `--plugin-dir` flag is recommended.

Build your first plugin from what you already have: a skill from world 3 and an MCP server from lesson 4 of this world. The transfer is literally copying folders into the plugin structure plus a six-line manifest. That's exactly what you'll do in this world's finale.

tasks.instruction: The plugin won't install. Find the error in the .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest.
tasks.explanation: The plugin name must be in kebab-case: lowercase, words separated by hyphens, no spaces or special characters — for example, "team-reports". "Team Reports Plugin!" with capital letters, spaces, and an exclamation mark is an invalid name. The other fields are fine: the description, version, and author are formatted correctly.

tasks.question: Where in a plugin must the plugin.json manifest be placed?
options: In the plugin root, next to the component folders
In the .claude-plugin/ folder inside the plugin
In the commands/ folder — it's configuration too
tasks.explanation: The manifest is the sole resident of the service folder: .claude-plugin/plugin.json. The components (skills/, commands/, agents/, hooks/, .mcp.json), on the other hand, live in the plugin root. Mixing up these two addresses is the most common mistake when building your first plugin.
tasks.hint: The manifest — in the service folder, the components — in the root. All that's left is to recall the name of the service folder.

tasks.instruction: The team-reports plugin is ready in the ./team-reports folder. Launch Claude Code with the local plugin (`claude --plugin-dir ./team-reports`), then make sure its MCP server came up (/mcp).

steps.output:
╭────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Claude Code                                │
│ Loaded local plugin: team-reports          │
│ + skill report-style                       │
│ + command /weekly                          │
│ + MCP server sales-data                    │
╰────────────────────────────────────────────╯
> _
steps.failHint: A local plugin is loaded with a flag at launch: claude --plugin-dir ./team-reports

steps.output:
MCP Servers:

  ✔ sales-data (plugin: team-reports) — connected
    Tools: get_sales (Sales for a month in YYYY-MM format)

  Total: 1 server, 1 tool.
steps.failHint: The status of MCP servers — including those brought up by a plugin — is shown by the /mcp slash command

tasks.successMessage: The plugin is loaded from a local folder: the skill, the command, and the MCP server all arrived in one package. This is what the development cycle looks like: edit the files → restart with --plugin-dir → check.

tasks.question: Choose ALL the component types that can be packaged into a single Claude Code plugin:
tasks.explanation: A plugin is a container for all five extension types: skills, commands, agents, hooks, and MCP servers, in any combination (all components are optional). A plugin does not contain a language model — the model is provided by Claude Code itself.
tasks.hint: There are five correct answers — recall the folder tree from the theory.

tasks.instruction: Assemble the sequence of actions: turn a ready skill and an MCP server into a working local plugin.
blocks: Create the plugin folder and, inside it, the service folder .claude-plugin/
Write the plugin.json manifest with a kebab-case name and a description
Lay out the components in the plugin root: the skill in skills/, the server in .mcp.json
Check: the skill fires, /mcp shows the server connected
distractors: Publish the folder to a marketplace right away — users will test it
tasks.explanation: The build order: skeleton → manifest → components → local run → check. Publishing without a local test is an antipattern: --plugin-dir exists precisely so marketplace users never see your drafts.
